questions.question: Which Core Web Vital is most likely irrelevant on a typical static Jekyll blog with no JavaScript framework?
questions.explanation: INP measures how quickly the page responds to interaction, which is mostly a JavaScript concern. A static blog without an interactive framework rarely has INP problems; LCP and CLS are where the wins are. FID has been replaced by INP and is no longer a Core Web Vital.

questions.question: Your hero image is causing a noticeable layout jolt as the page loads. What is the highest-leverage single fix?
choices: Add `width` and `height` attributes matching the intrinsic pixel dimensions.
Add a CSS `transition` so the shift is animated rather than instant.
questions.explanation: Setting `width` and `height` lets the browser compute the aspect-ratio box on first paint and reserve the correct space. Lazy-loading helps bandwidth but doesn't fix CLS - in fact, lazy-loading a hero image *hurts* LCP. AVIF reduces bytes but doesn't change layout behaviour.

questions.question: You self-host the body font and add `font-display: swap`. A reviewer says "but users will see the text style change as the font loads." How do you respond to this trade-off?
choices: Switch to `font-display: block` so users never see a style change.
Switch to `font-display: optional` so the web font is used only if cached, eliminating the swap.
Keep `swap`: invisible text during the font load is a worse user experience than a one-time style change, and `swap` is what avoids the FOIT.
Inline the entire font as base64 in the CSS to eliminate the load.
questions.explanation: `swap` accepts a brief visual swap (FOUT) in exchange for never blocking text rendering (FOIT). `block` reintroduces invisible text. `optional` is a valid choice for some sites but means most first-visit users never see your web font, which may not be what you want. Inlining the font bloats every page load.
